operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+RelEx in Chinese (1514066f3bb80490)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: RelEx in Chinese
Messages: 2
Date range: 25 Nov 2015 to 2015-11-26
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 1514066f3bb80490

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Roland Coeurjoly
Date: 25 Nov 2015

I have been reading the Engineering General Intelligence book and it mentions that RelEx was being implemented for the Chinese language. Is that part of the project still alive?

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-26

The Chinese port of RelEx has stalled alas, though might reboot next year depending on funding. One interesting approach would be to choose an existing Chinese dependency parser and write RelEx2Logic type rules transforming its output into OpenCog Atoms.

-- Ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Replacing TV and AV objects with property maps (possibly) (14ded849db14db4f)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Replacing TV and AV objects with property maps (possibly)
Messages: 5
Date range: 2015-06-13 to 2015-06-14
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14ded849db14db4f

============================================================

--- Message 1/5 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-06-13

Hi all,

Motivated by some stuff we want to do with SimpleCountTruthValue, but also more general considerations, Man Hin and I have been discussing the possibility of finally doing away with the TruthValue and AttentionValue objects and shifting to something like the PropertyMaps used in MTGL.

https://software.sandia.gov/trac/mtgl/wiki/PropertyMaps

The basic short-term motivation is to simplify the thicket of overlapping TV types that seem to have emerged lately. The TV types we have are logically interrelated via complex multiple inheritance relations, which is usually a sign some other design should be introduced...

Thoughts?

-- Ben

--- Message 2/5 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-13

As I mentioned before, the opencog atomspace already supports this. The support has been there since forever. Its called HandleMap. Look it up. No one uses it. I already provided 101 reasons why it is difficult to support, and the #1 most terrifying reason is that you have to provide serialize/deserialize functions for it.

Until you can provide a serialize-deserialize proposal, it is a non-starter.

I would also like to see a statement about why you think this would somehow make things BETTER than what we have today, instead of making things WORSE.

--linas

--- Message 3/5 ---
From: Joel Pitt
Date: 2015-06-14

One could use json/bson like any other document key/value db. If you want to use postgres as the backing store it already supports a binary json field with indexing.

Definitely don't waste time reinventing or reimplementing some adhoc serialization!

The complex bit is making the properties work with the existing atomspace indexes.

--- Message 4/5 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-06-14

Hi Linas,

As Joel pointed out, serializing and deserializing key-value tables is not hard and there are existing specific libraries for this. Choosing the best one would require some evaluation and testing.

We will write down a sketch of how the current maze of TV objects would be re-implemented in this context...

-- Ben

--- Message 5/5 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-06-14

Hi Joel,

Yes, the above is basically what Man Hin and I were thinking...

But I guess there are two separate discussions here
1) Would the proposed new/alternative approach be a good idea
2) How much work would it be to switch over?

I'd prefer to focus on these separately, because sometimes an exaggerated initial impression of (2) can inhibit proper appreciation of (1)...

-- Ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Request for comment pattern recognition. (14e4384e15aca93a)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Request for comment pattern recognition.
Messages: 14
Date range: 2015-06-30 to 2015-07-01
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14e4384e15aca93a

============================================================

--- Message 1/14 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-30

Ben,

And everyone clever at solving tough puzzles:

I've recently had some discussions regarding a port of AIML to the
atomspace.  Turns out this is an interesting problem. In one sense, it has
an easy solution; in another sense, it poses a couple of challenges that we
should solve in a general setting.  I did not quickly find a
fully-complete, fully elegant solution, and so I thought I should ask for
comments.

In a certain sense, AIML inverts the way the pattern matcher works: given a
single sentence, it asks which of many thousands of patterns match it. This
is "opposite" from the pattern matcher, which applies one query to
thousands of "facts".  In principle, reversing these to roles is easy, and
only requires some minor jiggling (reversal) of some of the pattern matcher
callbacks. [. [...]

--- Message 2/14 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-06-30

The obvious trick would be to have a special variable node called 
BlobVariableNode

WordSequenceLink
    WordNode "I"
    BlobVariableNode "$many"
    WordNode "you"

Maybe extending that to a full regex matcher could be useful as well. Like

WordSequenceLink
    WordNode "I"
    RegexPlusLink
       RegexDotNode
    WordNode "you"

which would correspond to the regex expression

I .+ you

Nil

--- Message 3/14 ---
From: William Ma
Date: 30 Jun 2015

oh, why does this sound so similar to Fuzzy Matching...

--- Message 4/14 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-30

Because you never explained fuzzy matching to me?  If you already solved it
or think hyou solved it, pleaseexplain it to me or point me at
documentation.

--linas

--- Message 5/14 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 30 Jun 2015

https://github.com/opencog/atomspace/tree/master/opencog/query/FuzzyMatch
http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Approximate_Pattern_Matching

--- Message 6/14 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-07-01

I think fuzzy matching is different from what Linas is now asking, actually

I mean: Fuzzy matching could solve the problem he's now posing in a
sloppy, approximate way; but what he's looking for is an exact
solution based on the logic of multi-word wild-cards...

--- Message 7/14 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-07-01

Hmmm... I thought of other solutions but so far, they would all be
more abstract, complicated and expensive than Nil's solution...

On Tue, Jun 30, 2015 at 4:49 PM, 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
<opencog@googlegroups.com> wrote:

--- Message 8/14 ---
From: Leung Man Hin
Date: 2015-07-01

I think the fuzzy pattern matcher is not capable to handle this.
Its current implementation, in short, is to pick different nodes from
the query as starters, send them one by one to the pattern matcher, capture
(almost) everything found by the pattern matcher, compare them with the
 [...]

--- Message 9/14 ---
From: Jeremy Zucker
Date: 2015-06-30

Hi folks,

What Linas is describing sounds like segment variable pattern matching,
described in Paradigms of AI Programming, with source code examples here:
http://www.norvig.com/paip/patmatch.lisp

Another implementation of a pattern matcher that uses segment variables can
 [...]

--- Message 10/14 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-07-01

Thanks Jeremey, Nil,

The opencog pattern matcher is far more sophisticated than what Norvig
describes; yet it has some built-in assumptions that makes a many-to-one
matching ... err ... inelegant.  Nil's suggestion is the "obvious" one, but
as one works through the design implications, and the data structure
implications, some of the inelegance comes through.  A lot of the
inelegance has to do with the concept of the linear array -- essentially
arrays or sequences or lists are kind-of inelegant in opencog atomese.
They're not "natural".

So I was sort-of free-associating and looking for new and crazy and
different ways of representing linear data in the atomspace.

--linas

--- Message 11/14 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-07-01

Linas,

Well in the vein of free-associating,  one can represent

"I * you"

as

"X so that 'X contains x1= I' and 'X contains x2=you' and  x1 is before x2"

i.e. as something like

ExistsLink $L1, $L2
   ANDLink
        EvaluationLink
            PredicateNode "subsequence or element of list"
            $L1
            $X
        EvaluationLink
            PredicateNode "subsequence or element of list"
            $L2
            $X
        EquivalenceLink
            $L1
            WordNode "you"
        EquivalenceLink
            $L2
            WordNode "I"
        EvaluationLink
            PredicateNode "sequential in list"
            ListLink
                $L1
                $L2

This approach could be extended to cases with multiple wild-cards...

 [...]

--- Message 12/14 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-07-01

The problem here is that OpenCog doesn't have
*sequence* in its base representation... so representing sequences on
top of graphs or logic relations requires intervention of an
additional layer, I guess... e.g. the list-processing predicates I've
suggested, or something else similar...

--- Message 13/14 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-07-01

Thanks Ben,

I briefly entertained something like the below, and  made use of the
GreaterThanLink.  I decided that it was too complicated.

There are two "implementation details" in the atomspace that I want to
leverage. One is that the outgoing set is a vector, and so atoms in the
outgoing set can be very quickly accessed.  The other is that the incoming
set is an actual C++ std::set, which I think is an RB-tree under the
covers.(we could change it to a hash table if we desired).

AIML uses a trie to hold the patterns that it is matching, and so I am
thinking "the opencog incoming set is almost like a trie, but the pattern
matcher does not make use of its trie-ness, and what should I change to
make it work more trie-like?"

 [...]

--- Message 14/14 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-07-01

Hmm, well in the vein of more brainstorming, you COULD explicitly just
represent a trie in the Atomspace ... see attached hasty diagram of a
trie containing "to" and "ten" (an example from the Wikipedia page you
linked to) ....  The nodes in the trie are instance nodes, e.g. t_123
is a CharacterInstanceNode inheriting from the CharacterNode for "t"

(the tries for AIML would use WordNodes not characternodes, but
whatever, you get it...)

In that case the pattern matcher would be searching for a certain
*path*, right?  So presumably the PM could explicitly exploit the trie
structure, e.g. if the query were

ANDLink
     Inheritance $T1 "t"
     Inheritance $T2 "e"
     Inheritance $T3 "n"
     FollowsLink $T1 $T2
     FollowsLink $T2 $T3
     FollowsLink $T3 T4

 [...]

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Required level of experience to contribute in ML related projects for GSOC 15 (14ad84576ddfb79c)

From: f2012563@pilani.bits-pilani.ac.in
Date: 25 Dec 2014

Hi, I am a 3rd year CS undergrad from BITS Pilani, India. I am interested in working on ML related projects. I have completed Machine Learning course on Coursera and would like to know whether I need more knowledge and experience.

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-01-11

Hi,

Sorry for the late response, but it appears that gmail failed to deliver any email from around Christmastime, until just today.

A number of open projects for opencog are listed here: http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Ideas

Another great way to learn about opencog is to pick one of the open bug reports, and just fix it. For example, we need a python maintainer.

--linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Resolving Current AllPythonModuleTests Failures (14b8f63023b68f97)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Resolving Current AllPythonModuleTests Failures
Messages: 4
Date range: 15 Feb 2015 to 2015-02-18
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14b8f63023b68f97

============================================================

--- Message 1/4 ---
From: Curtis M. Faith
Date: 15 Feb 2015

There are currently two failures in AllPythonModuleTests. 1) ImportError: No module named anaphora.agents.hobbs. 2) InductionRule_InheritanceLink.scm fails because ConceptNodes are in opposite order than expected.

--- Message 2/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-02-15

I don't think we have any particular policy about where python code should live. My personal preference is that the directory structure should reflect the function, and not the programming language. Thus, NLP code belongs in /opencog/nlp and not in /opencog/python/

Naively, I'd say fix the path so that it points to the right places, instead of moving files around.

-- Linas

--- Message 3/4 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-02-16

Regarding ordering: some links are OrderedLinks and others are UnorderedLinks. InheritanceLink is ordered so that the order of arguments matters.

-- Ben

--- Message 4/4 ---
From: Curtis Faith
Date: 2015-02-18

I fixed PYTHONPATH so the code works where it stands in opencog/nlp/ in the Pull Request I just submitted.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Re running Cogita (14d15be425905b10)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Re running Cogita
Messages: 2
Date range: 2015-05-02 to 2015-05-02
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14d15be425905b10

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-02

I am cc'ing Man Hin and Aaron on this reply, because it is relevant to them.

Hah. I don't know if I should laugh or cry. This is exactly what the early, first-version of cogita did, before I invented "triples". This is why I created the pattern matcher -- the pattern matcher did not exist before cogita.

In my first attempt, I used the PM to search the atomspace for hypergraphs that looked like the user question. This "worked", but was so narrow, that it was almost useless. If the question was "who hit the ball?" then the atomspace had to contain the sentence "Dave hit the ball" so that PM could unify who==Dave.

So for version 2 of cogita, I tried to solve this problem; the complicated code in the triples directory was an attempt to find equivalent structures.

 [...]

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Curtis Faith
Date: 2015-05-02

Best guy I know for this problem is the guy who taught me how to decompose in fractal. Kurt schmucker used to run apples atg visualization group before there was photoshop.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Re Slack Notifications from the OpenCog team for Sep 9th (14fb2e6c6b0eadd6)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Re Slack Notifications from the OpenCog team for Sep 9th
Messages: 2
Date range: 2015-09-09 to 9 Sep 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14fb2e6c6b0eadd6

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-09-09

Hi Amen,

A 'term' is any 'link', in the sense of 'term algebra'. In the pattern matcher, a 'clause' is a top-level term: a pattern is a single AndLink that wraps N clauses.

--linas

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 9 Sep 2015

Thanks

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Re S, O or another link type (14c4507688df6f6c)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Re S, O or another link type
Messages: 4
Date range: 2015-03-23 to 2015-03-23
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14c4507688df6f6c

============================================================

--- Message 1/4 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-23

Aaron, Linas, etc.,





I think Linas's point may be that: When these fancy theoretical

arguments are applied to deal with the actual breadth of natural

language, as opposed to carefully chosen example sentences, then they

lead to extraordinary complexity and a lot of conceptual perversity,

which seem to many of us more complex/annoying than the proliferation

of subtypes and disjuncts in the link grammar



This may be a matter of taste.



However, the perverse complexity of these phrase-structure grammar

constructs when applied to the scope of actual sentences, seems to

contradict the idea that PSG somehow offers a simple conceptual

framework that explains human language.   I.e., to me it has seemed

that considered "as natural philosophy", PSG is an even bigger mess

than depen

--- Message 2/4 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-23

Aaron,



***

But I don't know of any good evidence for denying the existence of

phrases!  As far as i can tell that's just a desire on the part of

some NLP people to make nature conform to the limitations of their

software!

***



Hmm, it's really not about denying that phrases exist or are

meaningful.  It's about wanting to view phrases as emergent

structures, from a lower layer of dependencies, rather than about

wanting to view phrases as "linguistically atomic."



Similarly arguing that chemical atoms are composed of particles,

doesn't constitute argument that chemical atoms don't exist - but it

does open up the mind to thinking about degenerate matter, in which

particles are combined in ways that don't involve chemical atoms ...

and about interactions btw degenerate matte

--- Message 3/4 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-23

PSG, phrase structure grammar, oops

--- Message 4/4 ---
From: Aaron Nitzkin
Date: 2015-03-23

Hmm. Actually, to me, intuitively, PSG seems much more elegant than LG.

Difference in aesthetics? How do you objectively measure elegance?  I

understand tensors (in a basic way), and therefore the generality of the

mathematical structure of the LG dictionary, however, it reminds me of your

foray

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+robot modeling environment with speech and movement synchronization (14c54db9cfdce056)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: robot modeling environment with speech and movement synchronization
Messages: 3
Date range: 25 Mar 2015 to 27 Mar 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14c54db9cfdce056

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Sergei Kaunov
Date: 25 Mar 2015

Dear all, I was at Beijing 2013 summer school. Being involved in a Kurchatov institute project on modelling of emotional behaviour, our task is to find a system to visualize at least upper body with head and hands, and to give it a voice with synchronization.

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-03-26

Hi Sergei,

The 'Eva head' is a fairly sophisticated model of a human head, with emotional expressions. It implements visemes, although we have not yet created the libraries needed to sync lip movements to audio.

The Eva head is modeled in Blender, but is controlled by ROS. The pipeline consists of a webcam, face extraction, and eva_behavior module that decides which faces to look at and what emotional gestures to make.

The easiest way to run the demo is to use the docker file at https://github.com/opencog/docker/tree/master/indigo/eva

--linas

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Sergei Kaunov
Date: 27 Mar 2015

Thanks for fast reply, Linas! Gestures and some embodiment are the features needed in our research. Our robot is ROS powered too. I should learn about Blender.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+scm-eval in the cogserver shell gone (14ea828cf11299e3)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: scm-eval in the cogserver shell gone
Messages: 3
Date range: 19 Jul 2015 to 2015-07-20
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14ea828cf11299e3

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Jim Rutt
Date: 19 Jul 2015

the opencog 'scm-eval' command used to work. Now it invokes the guile shell but doesn't execute the code. I used scm-eval in some python code that drove the telnet interface. So either the original functionality should be returned or the docs should be updated.

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-07-19

Hi Jim,

You can get equivalent functionality by saying 'scm (ConceptNode "XYZ")' followed by a control-D or ascii 0x4 character. Alternately, the sequence 
.
 (single dot on a line by itself). You don't even need to say scm.

To get no echo back, you can say 'scm hush (ConceptNode "XYZ")'. Useful for sending large files without garbage up the terminal.

--linas

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Jim Rutt
Date: 2015-07-20

trivial to have fixed in my code. At this point I'm more concerned with the lack of congruence with the docs. We should either put scm-eval back in or change the docs. BTW, py-eval works as expected. Adding back scm-eval would seem right to keep scheme and python on an equal footing.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Seek guidance, New developer (151f638670604a46)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Seek guidance, New developer
Messages: 2
Date range: 30 Dec 2015 to 2015-12-31
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 151f638670604a46

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Manmohit Rekhi
Date: 30 Dec 2015

Hi, I went through the wiki and find opencog to be an amazing organization. My area of interest lies in Machine Learning, Data Mining and AI. I am more inclined towards unsupervised learning.

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-12-31

A difficult but interesting project would be to apply unsupervised pattern mining (using the OpenCog Pattern Miner) to provide adaptive pruning for forward and backward chaining in inference.

First steps: 1) learn about the Pattern Matcher and URE, run some PLN examples; 2) learn about the Pattern Miner.

Another interesting project would be to pick up unsupervised grammar learning with Linas Vepstas.

-- Ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+simplified ECAN version 3 (14be2fb7f4a9fcc3)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: simplified ECAN version 3
Messages: 4
Date range: 2015-03-04 to 2015-03-04
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14be2fb7f4a9fcc3

============================================================

--- Message 1/4 ---
From: matt.ikle@gmail.com
Date: 2015-03-04

1) reworded wage calculations; 2) updated the rent updating equations. Comments welcome especially wrt rent updating. If these changes make sense then we could begin experimentation.

--- Message 2/4 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-04

Yeah, the new rent update equation makes more sense to me, and seems like it should probably be workable...

--- Message 3/4 ---
From: matt.ikle@gmail.com
Date: 2015-03-04

A perhaps major issue just occurred to me: the FocusBoundary s_AF is static, rather than dynamically determined. I'm thinking we want to adjust s_AF 'on the fly' but how best to do this? Do we want a certain proportion of Atoms in the AF?

--- Message 4/4 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-04

Well, if we do let s_AF vary, we may want upper and lower bounds on how many items can be in the AF. So maybe the boundary is set so that the AF contains the top N Atoms where N_min <= N <= N_max. We could identify a target percentage of total STI that the AF should have.

-- Ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Some OpenCog architecture thoughts (and I am back) (14e535eeeb5e76a0)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Some OpenCog architecture thoughts (and I am back)
Messages: 7
Date range: 2015-07-03 to 2015-07-06
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14e535eeeb5e76a0

============================================================

--- Message 1/7 ---
From: Cassio Pennachin
Date: 2015-07-03

I am getting involved with OpenCog again. I was there when we set on this adventure. Ben and Jim Rutt have tasked me with improving the design for single-machine performance, and improving development processes and practices. I discussed with Jim the concentric rings architecture view.

--- Message 2/7 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-07-03

An oversight regarding thread-safety is that the current 3DSpaceMap is presumably not thread-safe. The main structure inside 3DSpaceMap is an octree, and Yi-Shan or Brad are likely to replace it with an OctoMap which is thread-safe. The 3DSpaceMap will be important for perception synthesis in Hanson Robotics use of OpenCog.

--- Message 3/7 ---
From: Cassio Pennachin
Date: 2015-07-03

I ignored that issue and everything related to old embodiment since we are in the process of getting rid of it. Making it thread safe has to be done.

--- Message 4/7 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-07-03

I think the spacemap and closely associated code are the only substantial part of the old embodiment we need to keep.

--- Message 5/7 ---
From: Keyvan Mir Mohammad Sadeghi
Date: 2015-07-04

I skimmed through concentric rings architecture description, and it seemed more like another perspective on OpenCog rather than a design proposal, is that right?

--- Message 6/7 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-07-04

Yeah, that doc is a high level perspective, originally suggested by Jim Rutt and then fleshed out by Cassio, but the other second doc makes some concrete architecture suggestions.

--- Message 7/7 ---
From: Cassio Pennachin
Date: 2015-07-06

The concentric rings doc does not propose anything, it just frames the components in a systems oriented way.
